section.title: The Washing of the Disciples' Feet
section.sectionRationale: The Upper Room Discourse opens here. On the night He is betrayed, knowing His hour has come, Jesus rises from supper and washes His disciples' feet, the task of a slave, showing the full extent of His love and setting the pattern of servant greatness. He then reveals the betrayer, dismisses Judas into the night, and gives the new commandment to love one another as He has loved them, before foretelling Peter's denial.
section.thesis: On the eve of His death Jesus loves His own to the very end, stooping like a servant to wash their feet as both a cleansing they need and an example they must follow, and He gives them a new commandment to love one another as He has loved them, even as He unmasks the betrayer and foretells the failure of the boldest disciple.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Jesus washing the disciples' feet and Peter's protest; the meaning of the act and the call to serve one another; the unmasking and departure of Judas into the night; and the new commandment to love, with the prediction of Peter's denial.

units.label: Jesus washes their feet
units.note: He loved them to the very end

verses.text: It was now just before the Passover Feast, and Jesus knew that His hour had come to leave this world and return to the Father. Having {{agapao|loved}} His own who were in the world, He loved them to the very end.
The evening meal was underway, and the devil had already put into the heart of Judas, the son of Simon Iscariot, to betray Jesus.
Jesus knew that the Father had delivered all things into His hands, and that He had come from God and was returning to God.
So He got up from the supper, laid aside His outer garments, and wrapped a towel around His waist.
After that, He poured water into a basin and began to wash the disciples’ feet and dry them with the towel that was around Him.
He came to Simon Peter, who asked Him, “Lord, are You going to wash my feet?”
Jesus replied, “You do not realize now what I am doing, but later you will understand.”
“Never shall You wash my feet!” Peter told Him. Jesus answered, “Unless I wash you, you have no part with Me.”
“Then, Lord,” Simon Peter replied, “not only my feet, but my hands and my head as well!”
Jesus told him, “Whoever has already bathed needs only to wash his feet, and he will be completely clean. And you are clean, though not all of you.”
For He knew who would betray Him. That is why He said, “Not all of you are clean.”

units.label: An example to follow
units.note: if I your Lord have washed your feet

verses.text: When Jesus had washed their feet and put on His outer garments, He reclined with them again and asked, “Do you know what I have done for you?
You call Me Teacher and Lord, and rightly so, because I am.
So if I, your Lord and Teacher, have washed your feet, you also should wash one another’s feet.
I have set you an example so that you should do as I have done for you.
Truly, truly, I tell you, no servant is greater than his master, nor is a messenger greater than the one who sent him.
If you know these things, you will be blessed if you do them.

units.label: The betrayer revealed
units.note: Judas went out into the night

verses.text: I am not speaking about all of you; I know whom I have chosen. But this is to fulfill the Scripture: ‘The one who shares My bread has lifted up his heel against Me.’
I am telling you now before it happens, so that when it comes to pass, you will believe that I am He.
Truly, truly, I tell you, whoever receives the one I send receives Me, and whoever receives Me receives the One who sent Me.”
After Jesus had said this, He became troubled in spirit and testified, “Truly, truly, I tell you, one of you will betray Me.”
The disciples looked at one another, perplexed as to which of them He meant.
One of His disciples, the one whom Jesus loved, was reclining at His side.
So Simon Peter motioned to him to ask Jesus which one He was talking about.
Leaning back against Jesus, he asked, “Lord, who is it?”
Jesus answered, “It is the one to whom I give this morsel after I have dipped it.” Then He dipped the morsel and gave it to Judas son of Simon Iscariot.
And when Judas had taken the morsel, Satan entered into him. Then Jesus said to Judas, “What you are about to do, do quickly.”
But no one at the table knew why Jesus had said this to him.
Since Judas kept the money bag, some thought that Jesus was telling him to buy what was needed for the feast, or to give something to the poor.
As soon as he had received the morsel, Judas went out into the night.

units.label: A new commandment
units.note: love one another as I have loved you

verses.text: When Judas had gone out, Jesus said, “Now the Son of Man is {{doxazo|glorified}}, and God is glorified in Him.
If God is glorified in Him, God will also glorify the Son in Himself, and will glorify Him at once.
Little children, I am with you only a little while longer. You will look for Me, and as I said to the Jews, so now I say to you: ‘Where I am going, you cannot come.’
A new {{entole|commandment}} I give you: Love one another. As I have loved you, so you also must love one another.
By this everyone will know that you are My disciples, if you love one another.”
“Lord, where are You going?” Simon Peter asked. Jesus answered, “Where I am going, you cannot follow Me now, but you will follow later.”
“Lord,” said Peter, “why can’t I follow You now? I will lay down my life for You.”
“Will you lay down your life for Me?” Jesus replied. “Truly, truly, I tell you, before the rooster crows, you will deny Me three times.

agapao.gloss: To love with self-giving love. John says Jesus loved His own to the very end, or to the uttermost, a love that stoops to wash feet and will go all the way to the cross. This same love becomes the pattern and power of the new commandment: as I have loved you, so love one another. The love is defined by Christ's own sacrificial action.

doxazo.gloss: To glorify. Once Judas leaves to set the betrayal in motion, Jesus says, now the Son of Man is glorified. The glorification is His death, resurrection, and exaltation, all set in motion by this hour. In John the cross is not Jesus' shame but His glory and the glory of God shown in Him.

entole.gloss: Commandment. Jesus gives a new commandment, to love one another. It is new not because love was unknown, but in its measure and ground: as I have loved you. Christ's self-giving love is both the standard and the source, and such love is the badge by which the world will recognize His disciples.

basic.context: On the night before He dies, at the Passover meal, Jesus knows His hour has come. Fully aware that all authority is His and that He is returning to the Father, He does the unthinkable: He rises, wraps a towel around His waist like a slave, and washes His disciples' feet. Peter protests, then over-asks, and Jesus teaches that this washing is both a cleansing they need and an example they must imitate. Jesus then reveals that one of them will betray Him, gives Judas the morsel, and sends him out into the night. To those who remain He gives a new commandment: love one another as He has loved them. Then He foretells that Peter will deny Him three times.
basic.hermeneutics: The foot washing works on two levels. It is a picture of the deeper cleansing Jesus gives, without which no one has a part with Him, and it is an example of humble, serving love to be copied. The key questions are, will we receive Jesus' cleansing, and will we serve as He served. The new commandment raises the bar to the measure of Christ's own love, the very love that is about to be shown at the cross. And Peter's coming denial warns against self-confident boasting.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is framed by love. Jesus agapao, loved His own to the very end, a love that stoops and will go to the cross. Once Judas leaves, He says the Son of Man is now doxazo, glorified, His death being His glory. And He gives a new entole, commandment, to love one another as He has loved them.
basic.typology: The foot washing enacts the greater cleansing from sin that Jesus provides, hinting at baptism and the once-for-all bathing of those who are His. The Passover setting points to Jesus the Lamb whose death delivers His people. Judas's betrayal fulfills the Scripture of the friend who turns against the psalmist.
basic.christ: Christ is the Lord and Teacher who serves like a slave, the one who cleanses His people so they may have a part with Him, and whose love to the end sets the pattern for His church. He is glorified in His hour and gives the command that marks His followers.
basic.god: God is glorified in the Son, and the Son in the Father. The Father has given all things into the Son's hands, and the whole scene of humble love unfolds within that secure knowledge of God's sovereign purpose.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 41 foretells the betrayer who shared bread. Philippians 2 describes Christ taking the form of a servant. 1 John 3 and 4 unfold love as the mark of God's children. Luke 22 records Peter's denial predicted.
basic.oneStory: The Lord of all stoops to wash feet and to lay down His life, showing that true greatness in God's kingdom is servant love. His cleansing gives His people a part with Him, and His new commandment forms a community whose love for one another will show the world the love of God poured out at the cross.

context.literary: The opening of the Upper Room Discourse, chapters 13 to 17, which unfolds Jesus' farewell to His disciples. The foot washing is a prophetic sign-act interpreted by Jesus' own words, followed by the revelation of the betrayer, the departure of Judas, the new commandment, and the prediction of Peter's denial. The repeated notes of Jesus' knowledge, of His hour, His authority, and the betrayer, frame the whole in divine sovereignty and love.
context.historical: Washing guests' feet, dusty from sandaled travel, was the task of the lowest slave; for a host or teacher to do it was startling. Reclining at table on couches explains how the beloved disciple could lean back against Jesus. Sharing a dipped morsel was a gesture of honor, making Jesus' gift to Judas poignant. The night motif underscores the darkness into which Judas departs and the coming hour of trial.
context.canonical: The foot washing enacts the servant humility of Philippians 2:5-8 and the cleansing from sin central to the New Testament. The new commandment stands with 1 John's teaching that love is the mark of the born-again and with 1 Corinthians 13. Judas's betrayal fulfills Psalm 41:9, and the prediction of Peter's denial is shared with the Synoptic accounts, underscoring both human frailty and Jesus' foreknowledge.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To open the farewell by showing the full extent of Jesus' love in servant humility and cleansing, to command His disciples to love and serve one another as He has loved them, and to frame His coming death as glorification, foretold and freely embraced amid betrayal and denial.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The foot washing is both a unique cleansing sign and a prescriptive example; Jesus explicitly commands His followers to do as He has done. The new commandment to love one another as Christ loved is binding on the church in every age, while the specific predictions about Judas and Peter are descriptive of that night.

debates.question: Is the foot washing chiefly an example of humility or a picture of cleansing from sin?

views.label: A picture of spiritual cleansing
views.case: Jesus tells Peter, unless I wash you, you have no part with Me, and distinguishes the once-for-all bath from the daily washing of the feet, pointing to the cleansing from sin that only He provides.

views.label: An example of humble service
views.case: Jesus interprets the act by saying He has set an example, so that His disciples should wash one another's feet; the emphasis falls on imitating His servant love.

debates.bearing: The passage holds both: the washing signifies the cleansing believers must receive from Christ, and it models the humble, serving love they must show one another, so cleansing received overflows in service given.

debates.question: What makes the commandment to love one another new?

views.label: New in its measure and pattern
views.case: Love of neighbor was already commanded, but the standard is now as I have loved you, the self-giving love shown in the foot washing and the cross, a measure never before set.

views.label: New in its covenant setting and power
views.case: It belongs to the new covenant Jesus inaugurates, empowered by the Spirit He will send, so the command comes with the enabling love of God poured into the heart.

debates.bearing: Both enrich the point: the commandment is new in raising love to the measure of Christ's own sacrifice and in coming with the new covenant power to fulfill it, so that such love becomes the visible mark of His disciples.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is bracketed by love. John says that Jesus, having agapao, loved His own who were in the world, loved them to the very end, or to the uttermost, a love that stoops to the slave's basin and will go all the way to the cross. When Judas departs to trigger the betrayal, Jesus declares that now the Son of Man is doxazo, glorified, for in John the cross is the hour of glory, not of shame. And to those who remain He gives a new entole, commandment, to love one another as He has loved them, a love whose measure is His own self-giving and whose presence is the badge by which the world will know His disciples.

typology.fulfillment: Christ's humble self-emptying and the cleansing from sin He provides, without which no one has a part with Him, John 13:8 and Philippians 2:5-8.
Jesus the true Passover Lamb, whose death on this night delivers His people, John 13:1.

typology.type: The friend who shared bread and betrayed, Psalm 41:9
typology.fulfillment: Judas, who ate with Jesus and lifted his heel against Him, John 13:18.

study.christ: Christ, knowing that all things are in His hands and that He comes from God and returns to God, uses that very security to stoop and wash His disciples' feet, the task of a slave. He is the Lord and Teacher who serves, showing that in His kingdom greatness bends low in love. He is the one who cleanses His people so that they may have a part with Him, the once-for-all bath that makes them clean. He embraces His hour as glorification, foretells His betrayal and His disciple's denial without flinching, and gives the new commandment whose measure is His own love, soon to be poured out at the cross.
study.god: God is glorified in the Son, and the Son in the Father, so that the humble service and coming death of Jesus are the arena where God's glory shines brightest. The Father has delivered all things into the Son's hands, and within that sovereign security the Son freely loves and serves. The whole scene reveals a God whose glory is not distant majesty but self-giving love that stoops to wash feet and to die, and who calls His people into a community marked by that same love, by which the world will know they belong to Him.

crossReferences.note: Christ took the form of a servant and humbled Himself to death, the mind enacted in the foot washing.
Even my close friend who ate my bread has lifted his heel against me, fulfilled in Judas.
By this we know love, that He laid down His life for us, the measure of the new commandment.
If God so loved us, we also ought to love one another, unfolding love one another as I have loved you.

study.oneStory: The Lord of all, with every authority in His hands, kneels to wash His disciples' feet and prepares to lay down His life, revealing that true greatness in God's kingdom is servant love. The cleansing He gives grants His people a part with Him, and the new commandment forms a community whose love for one another mirrors and displays the love of God poured out at the cross. Even as betrayal and denial gather in the shadows, Jesus loves His own to the very end, and calls them to love as He has loved, so that the watching world may know the God who stooped so low.

quiz.q: What did Jesus reveal by washing His disciples' feet on the night of His betrayal?

options.text: The full extent of His love, stooping like a servant to cleanse and to set an example.
options.note: Verses 1 to 15 tie the act to His love, the cleansing, and the example.

options.text: That He no longer regarded Himself as their Lord.
options.note: He affirms He is their Lord and Teacher, and serves anyway, verse 13.

options.text: That good hosts should provide for their guests' comfort.
options.note: It is far more than etiquette; it reveals His love and models servant humility.

options.text: His humility in taking the role of a servant.
options.note: True, but it omits the deeper meaning of cleansing and the love shown to the very end.

quiz.q: What did Jesus mean when He told Peter, unless I wash you, you have no part with Me?

options.text: The washing pictures the cleansing from sin that only Christ gives, essential to belonging to Him.
options.note: Verse 8 points beyond the feet to the spiritual cleansing Jesus provides.

options.text: Peter's feet were unusually dirty and needed extra attention.
options.note: The issue is not literal dirt but the cleansing Christ alone can give.

options.text: Peter had to accept the service to remain a disciple.
options.note: There is a call to receive, but the deeper point is the cleansing from sin, not merely accepting a courtesy.

options.text: Without receiving from Christ, one cannot share in Him.
options.note: True, but it does not name that the washing pictures cleansing from sin.

quiz.q: What new commandment does Jesus give His disciples?

options.text: To love one another as He has loved them, the mark of His disciples.
options.note: Verses 34 and 35 give the command and its measure, as I have loved you.

options.text: To keep the whole law of Moses more strictly.
options.note: The new commandment is to love as Christ loved, not stricter law-keeping.

options.text: To love their neighbors as they already knew to do.
options.note: It is new in its measure, as I have loved you, not merely the old love-of-neighbor.

options.text: To love one another sincerely and truly.
options.note: True, but it omits the crucial standard, as I have loved you, and the witness it gives.

quiz.q: How does Jesus speak of His coming death after Judas leaves?

options.text: As the glorification of the Son of Man, in which God is glorified.
options.note: Verses 31 and 32 frame the cross as glory, not defeat.

options.text: As a tragic accident that has spun out of control.
options.note: Jesus speaks of it as glorification, fully within God's purpose.

options.text: As a necessary suffering He must simply endure.
options.note: He does embrace the suffering, but John frames it as glorification, not mere grim endurance.

options.text: As the moment when His mission is completed.
options.note: True in part, but it misses the specific note that it is His and the Father's glorification.

quiz.q: How does Jesus respond to Peter's promise to lay down his life?

options.text: He foretells that Peter will deny Him three times before the rooster crows.
options.note: Verse 38 records the prediction of Peter's denial.

options.text: He praises Peter for his unshakable loyalty.
options.note: Jesus instead foretells Peter's failure, exposing his overconfidence.

options.text: He tells Peter he cannot follow now but will follow later.
options.note: Jesus does say this, but His direct response to Peter's boast is the prediction of the threefold denial.

options.text: He gently warns that Peter's confidence will fail that very night.
options.note: True in substance, but it omits the specific prediction of three denials before the rooster crows.

questions: Jesus, fully secure in who He was, used that security to stoop and serve. How might resting in your identity in Christ free you to serve others without fear?
Peter first refused the washing, then over-asked. Where do you resist simply receiving Christ's cleansing on His terms?
Jesus commanded love measured by His own, as I have loved you. Whose love are you finding hard to extend, and how does Christ's love for you reshape that?
The world is meant to know Christ's disciples by their love for one another. What would change in your church relationships if that were truly the aim?
Peter boasted of his loyalty and soon failed. Where might quiet dependence on Christ serve you better than confident promises about your own faithfulness?

translationNotes.note: He loved them to the very end; the BSB footnote gives or He showed them the full extent of His love. The phrase means both to the end of His life and to the uttermost degree.
The one who shares My bread has lifted up his heel against Me quotes Psalm 41:9, fulfilled in Judas's betrayal.
The one whom Jesus loved was reclining at His side; the BSB footnote notes the Greek is in the bosom of Jesus, reflecting the posture of reclining at table.
The BSB footnote notes some manuscripts omit the clause if God is glorified in Him; the sense of the Son's swift glorification is unchanged.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 13:1-38. Verse text assembled by concatenating content segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped, embedded speaker turns joined with single spaces. The em dash in verse 32 (in Himself, and will glorify Him) was converted to a comma. No em or en dashes remain. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
